---
title: "Comparison of Statistical Techniques"
date: "2026-03-28"
description: "Which estimator is the most appropriate?"
categories: [ggplot, FibFreq, Analysis, DataViz]
image: "index_files/figure-html/plot_boxplots_frequency_estimates-1.png"
---
In data science we are often confronted with different estimators for the same quantity. For instance, there are various methodes for calculating the fundamental frequency of an oscillatory time series or for the strength of long-range persistence of a noisy time series. It is common practise to benchmark the different estimators for a specific suite of test data. How to compare the results? In this post, I will present different vizualizations of the statistical summary.

As test data I will use realizations of a model for stochastic oscillations. Each time series contains 1000 data points
sampled at 1 kHz leading to time series of 1 second length. 

```{r setup_and_load_packages, message = FALSE}
# Loading packages
packages <- c("ggplot2", "tibble", "scico", "tidyr", "purrr", "dplyr")

# Install packages not yet installed
installed_packages <- packages %in% rownames(installed.packages())
if (any(installed_packages == FALSE)) {
  install.packages(packages[!installed_packages])
}
invisible(lapply(packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

if (!require(FibFreq)) {
  library(devtools)
  install_github("origami-dss/FibFreq")
}
library(FibFreq)
```


First I define a function that computes realizations of stochastic oscillators:

```{r define_ts_model}
Gaussian_distributed_ts_with_Gaussian_PSD <- function(
  T = 1,
  f_mean = 22.5,
  f_sigma = 2,
  delta_t = 0.001,
  seed = NULL
) {
  fs <- 1. / delta_t
  N <- T / delta_t
  N_2 <- floor(N / 2)

  set.seed(seed)
  x <- rnorm(N)
  X <- fft(x, inverse = FALSE)
  freq <- (0:N_2) / N / delta_t

  coeffs_FourAmps <- sqrt(dnorm(freq, f_mean, f_sigma))
  if (N %% 2 == 0) {
    multiplyers = c(
      coeffs_FourAmps,
      (rev(coeffs_FourAmps))[-c(1, length(coeffs_FourAmps))]
    )
  } else {
    multiplyers = c(coeffs_FourAmps, rev(coeffs_FourAmps))
  }

  X_f <- multiplyers * X
  x_ts <- Re(fft(X_f, inverse = TRUE) / length(X_f))
}
```

Let me visualize three examples of the simulated time series:

```{r plot_model_ts_stochastic_oscillations}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
#| 
x_1 <- Gaussian_distributed_ts_with_Gaussian_PSD(seed = 1)
x_2 <- Gaussian_distributed_ts_with_Gaussian_PSD(seed = 2)
x_3 <- Gaussian_distributed_ts_with_Gaussian_PSD(seed = 3)

ecg1 <- tibble(no = 1, time = 1:1000, ecg = x_1)
ecg2 <- tibble(no = 2, time = 1:1000, ecg = x_2)
ecg3 <- tibble(no = 3, time = 1:1000, ecg = x_3)

data_fig_ecgs = bind_rows(ecg1, ecg2, ecg3)

data_fig_ecgs %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = ecg)) +
  geom_line(col = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(slope = 0, intercept = 0, linewidth = 0.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_grid(rows = vars(no), scales = "free", labeller = label_parsed) +
  labs(
    x = "Time [ms]",
    y = "",
    title = "Three modelled Stochastic Oscillations with a Frequency of 22.5 Hz",
    caption = paste(
      "Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n",
      format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y")
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(-2, 1010),
    expand = c(0.0, 0),
    breaks = (0:5) * 200
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 16),
    strip.text.y = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "line"),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank()
  )
```

In a second step, this function is used to generate 500 time series. The fundamental frequency is then computed for each series using five different estimators based on functions from the ***FibFreq*** package.

```{r create_data_set}
N <- 500
model_data <- map_dfc(
  .x = 1:N,
  .f = function(x) {
    out <- Gaussian_distributed_ts_with_Gaussian_PSD(seed = x)
    setNames(as.data.frame(out), paste0("ts_", x))
  }
)

fn <- function(x, delta_t = 0.001, f_min = 1, f_max = 45) {
  res_sin_fit <- freq_fitted_sinusoidals(
    x,
    delta_t = delta_t,
    test_freqs = seq(f_min, f_max, by = 0.1)
  )
  res_max2max <- freq_max2max(x, delta_t = delta_t)
  res_thresh <- freq_threshold_crossing(x, delta_t)
  res_MaxP <- freq_argmax_periodogram(x, delta_t, f_min = f_min, f_max = f_max)
  names(res_MaxP)[1] <- "freq_MaxP"
  res_Lorentz <- freq_Lorentz_fit(
    x,
    delta_t = delta_t,
    f_min = f_min,
    f_max = f_max
  )
  return(c(
    res_sin_fit[1],
    res_max2max[1],
    res_thresh[1],
    res_MaxP[1],
    res_Lorentz[1]
  ))
}

result_frequency_estimates <- as_tibble(model_data) %>% map_df(~ fn(.x))

freq_res_for_boxplot <- as_tibble(cbind(
  no = 1:N,
  result_frequency_estimates
)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = !no, names_to = "names", values_to = "freq_estimate")
```

Finally, I visualize the results: each estimated frequency is represented as a gray dot, with the five estimators vertically aligned. Boxplots provide a clear graphical summary for each estimator.

```{r plot_boxplots_frequency_estimates}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Results of the benchmark: 
#|   The fundamental frequency was estimated using five different methods 
#|   applied to a model dataset of 500 oscillatory time series with a prescribed frequency of 22.5 Hz 
#|   (red vertical line). 
#|   Individual frequency estimates (from top to bottom, normalized number of zero crossings, 
#|   maximum of the periodogram, normalized number of maxima, Lorentz fit to the Fourier amplitudes, 
#|   frequency of the best fitting sinus function) are shown as grey dots. 
#|   Their statistical distributions are summarized by boxplots, 
#|   the diamond indicates the mean, the horizontal line within each box marks the median, 
#|   and the box boundaries represent the 25th and 75th percentiles. 
#|   Whiskers extend from the 5th to the 95th percentile. 
COLS = scico(5, palette = 'oleron')

f_box <- function(x) {
  r <- quantile(x, probs = c(0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.95))
  names(r) <- c("ymin", "lower", "middle", "upper", "ymax")
  r
}

freq_res_for_boxplot %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = freq_estimate, y = names, fill = names)) +
  geom_vline(
    aes(xintercept = 22.5),
    col = "indianred3",
    linewidth = 3,
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_jitter(
    width = 0.001,
    height = 0.2,
    size = 0.5,
    col = "grey60",
    alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun.data = f_box,
    geom = "boxplot",
    alpha = 0.6,
    width = 0.6,
    outliers = TRUE,
    linewidth = 0.5,
    fill = COLS
  ) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = 'point', shape = 18, size = 3) +
  labs(
    x = "Estimated Frequency (Hz)",
    title = "Benchmarks of Frequency Estimation Techniques",
    caption = paste(
      "Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n",
      format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y")
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(15, 25, by = 5),
    minor_breaks = seq(16, 32, by = 1)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    labels = c(
      "freq_threshold_crossing" = expression(f[theta]),
      "freq_MaxP" = expression(f[MaxP]),
      "freq_max2max" = expression(f[Max2Max]),
      "freq_Lorentz" = expression(f[Lorentz]),
      "freq_fitted" = expression(f[BestFitting])
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 16),
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(hjust = 0),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 6),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )
```

This plot highlights the importance of showing both, the individual estimates (grey dots) and their statistical summary: the boxplots alone do not capture the discrete outcomes of $f_{MaxP}$ and $f_{BestFitting}$.
